summary: Validated SplashScreen behavior with real Playwright on Chromium. Splash renders on initial load, all key elements (title, subtitle, description, Entrar button, badge) are visible with opacity=1 and visibility=visible after CSS animations resolve (~2.2s). Clicking 'Entrar' fades out and removes the splash within ~1s, restoring body overflow and revealing the Dashboard (data-testid=dashboard-page). Auto-close also works correctly at ~4.4s without user interaction. Post-dismiss the app is fully interactive: scroll restored, sidebar nav works, global search trigger clickable, Jugadores route loads with data.
critical_code_review_comments: SplashScreen.jsx: close() is defined inline and referenced in setTimeout(close, 4000) inside useEffect, but is also called from onClick. Logic is fine, however the eslint-disable on the dependency array hides the fact that close has no dependencies that change—safe. No action needed.

splash.css: All animations correctly use 'both' fill-mode and end with opacity:1 — verified at runtime that title/subtitle/description/button reach opacity=1 (no stuck-at-0 issue). The artifact previously seen by the main agent was a screenshot-tool rendering quirk, not a real bug.

Minor: setTimeout duration (4000ms) + fade transition (700ms) sums to ~4.7s while the loading-bar CSS animation runs 3.8s — they are visually close enough but could be aligned for consistency (cosmetic only).
context_for_next_testing_agent: Splash screen fully validated. Both manual click on Entrar (data-testid=splash-enter-btn) and 4s auto-close dismiss work correctly. document.body.style.overflow is properly toggled hidden during splash and restored after. Dashboard (data-testid=dashboard-page) and sidebar nav (data-testid=sidebar-nav-*) become interactive immediately after dismissal. No CSS opacity-stuck bug — animations resolve to opacity=1 on real browsers.
rca of the issue: No bug found. Main agent's concern that title/Entrar might be stuck at opacity 0 due to CSS animation fill-mode was unfounded — 'both' fill-mode correctly retains the end state (opacity:1). Confirmed by computed-style probe in real Chromium.
